annotation.text: Good evening, everyone.
My name is Jason Purcell, and I'm the communications officer at the Canadian Literature Centre.
It is an honour to welcome you to the beautiful Winspear Centre for the 2016 CLC Kreisel
Lecture, delivered by none other than Margaret Atwood.
Yeah, that's right.
So many of us here, I'm sure, have been inspired by Ms. Atwood's concern with environment,
land and place in the formation of communities.
So before we begin, I'd like to take this opportunity to acknowledge our location on
the traditional territories and lands of Treaty 6 and the Métis Nation of Alberta Region
4, and to thank the diverse Indigenous peoples whose footsteps have marked this territory
for centuries, such as Cree, Salto, Blackfoot, Métis, Nakota Sioux, Chippewan, Assiniboine,
the Pappas Chase and their descendants.
A decade ago, the CLC came into being as a national centre for scholarly inquiry into
and celebration of Canadian writing in all of its diverse forms.
The CLC would not exist if not for our founding benefactor, Dr. Eric Schloss, and we couldn't
have done it either without support from the University of Alberta's Faculty of Arts and
the Office of the Dean of Arts.
So thank you.
Several supporters and friends have contributed over the years to the CLC's well-being and
helped strengthen Edmonton's and indeed Canada's literary community.
Let's give a round of applause to all of this evening's supporters.
Now this evening marks the 10th anniversary CLC Kreisel Lecture, as well as a decade of
reading, writing and research.
It means a great deal to us to see so many of you here celebrating with the CLC.
We have a suspicion that this is a moment we'll all want to revisit, so we have it covered.
We're excited to once again be partnering with CBC Radio 1 Ideas with Paul Kennedy,
who are recording this evening's lecture to be broadcast in the fall.
And for those of you who can't wait to add another Atwood book to your collection, keep
an eye out for the print publication of this evening's lecture, co-published by the University
of Alberta Press and the CLC, and out next spring.
Finally, it shouldn't be a surprise to you, but we are in the presence of one of the great
tweeters of our time.
In honour of this, we invite you to take out your phones, silence them and get ready to
tweet.
Be sure to follow us at CLCUAlberta and use the hashtag Kreisel2016 to join the conversation.
I know a lot of you use Twitter.
Finally, it is my pleasure to introduce Dr. Lise Gautel, the Acting Dean of Arts, who
will offer greetings from the Faculty of Arts.
Dr. Gautel is Professor of Women's and Gender Studies, Vice Chair of the Board of the Women's
Legal Education and Action Fund, and is on the board of the Centre for Constitutional
Studies.
Dr. Gautel is co-author of Bad Attitudes on Trial, Feminism, Pornography and the Butler
Decision, and co-editor of Open Boundaries, a Canadian Women's Studies reader.
Please join me in welcoming to the stage, Dean Gautel.
Thank you, Jason.
Welcome on behalf of the Faculty of Arts at the University of Alberta, and what a wonderful
way to end such a beautiful spring day.
It's much nicer here than it is even in Toronto for anyone who's visiting us tonight from
Toronto.
A special welcome to our new Board Chair, Michael Fair.
The Faculty of Arts wishes you our most sincere congratulations in your new role.
Welcome also to the University Board of Governors Vice Chair, Shanaz Shiraz.
The Kreisel Lecture is successful precisely because of strong partnerships.
Welcome to the U of A Press.
As Jason was mentioning, the press co-publishes the Kreisel series with the CLC.
In addition, we're pleased to have CBC Radio Ideas here tonight, recording the lecture
for a broadcast this fall.
As many of you know, the Kreisel Lecture is an annual event hosted by our Canadian Literature
Centre.
This lecture series was established in memory of Professor Henry Kreisel.
Professor Kreisel was Chair of English and Vice President Academic in the 1960s and 70s.
Professor Kreisel was an award-winning teacher who inspired a love of the written word in
his students.
He taught the first courses on Canadian literature at U of A.
It's surprising really to think of a time when Canadian literature was not central to
the curriculum of Canadian English departments.
I'm sure that Professor Kreisel would feel especially honoured with tonight's talk.
Professor Kreisel was Austrian-born and Jewish.
He was interned in Canada as an enemy alien during the Second World War.
The huge irony of that.
He was one of the very first people to introduce the immigrant experience into Canadian literature.
The annual Kreisel Lecture is not only an annual celebration of Canadian writing, but
a recognition of the importance of public intellectualism.
In the Faculty of Arts, we have a very strong commitment to nurturing public conversations.
This is an exciting anniversary, so join me in wishing happy 10th anniversary to this
speaker series as well as to the Canadian Literature Centre.
In the Faculty of Arts, we're proud to be the home of the CLC.
This successful centre tackles key societal issues in its role as the Western hub of the
Canadian literary community.
It also works to bring together authors, readers, researchers, students, and teachers from across
the country.
The CLC also continues to publish the highly acclaimed 18 Bridges Literary Magazine, which
I'm sure many of you have read.
Before I conclude my remarks, I'd like to say a quick thank you to all at the CLC who
made this possible, Dr. Marie Carrière and her team, Jason Purcell, and to you, Warren
Sola.
Of course, we also need to thank Eric and Alexis Schloss for their generous founding
gift of the CLC and also for their immense continued support of the centre over the
past decade.
With that, I'd like to turn over the microphone to Dr. Marie Carrière, the Director of the
Canadian Literature Centre, who has the great honour of introducing Margaret Atwood.
Good evening.
I am deeply honoured to be introducing Margaret Atwood.
As Dean Gautel just mentioned, this is a milestone anniversary and event for the CLC, and I thank
everyone here this evening for being part of our celebration.
Edmonton's own arts magazine, The Yards, perhaps said it best in these past weeks leading up
to tonight's event, who better for the Canadian Literature Centre to invite for its 10th annual
Kreisel Lecture than the country's best-known author?
Who better indeed?
Margaret Atwood is not only Canada's best-known author, as well as one of the most productive
writers of her time.
As a novelist, outspoken environmentalist, feminist, poet, librettist, critic, and satirist,
she cultivates an international reputation.
As many of us here will recall, and as we will very soon have the treat to discover
further in tonight's lecture, Margaret Atwood is one of the very founders of what we consider
to be Canadian literature today.
Margaret Atwood's first collection of poetry, Double Persephone, appeared in 1961.
The self-published book, handset and designed by the author herself, went on to win the
E.J.
Pratt Medal.
The rest, as they say, is history.
If the concerns with the doubleness and revival marked the early forays into poetry, Margaret
Atwood's exploration of what she termed as Canada's paranoid schizophrenia in the journals
of Susanna Moody also foreshadowed her deep interests in Canadian culture and historiography,
which she would pursue throughout her writing career.
I am a word in a foreign language, wrote Atwood's very own pioneering Mrs. Moody in 1970, speaking
from the haunted underground of settler invader identity crisis and survival.
Women draw a huge figure in the historical as well as narrative landscapes of Atwoodian
fiction, whether they are pulled back into the northern wilderness of their childhood,
as in the novels surfacing, or fighting on the battleground of femininity and all of
its trappings, as in the novels, The Edible Woman, Lady Oracle, Cat's Eye, and The Robber
Bride.
Unruly female heroines have since emerged to disrupt the nation and its colonial mindset,
including the unforgettable Grace Marks of Alias Grace and Iris Chase Griffin of the
Booker Prize winning novel, The Blind Assassin.
The gothic, macabre, or disorderly aspects of these women have not been lost on the variable
plethora of literary criticism written on Atwood's work, nor has Margaret Atwood's own
literary use of birds and animals gone unanalyzed.
The hybrid, often female, human-animal forms, the insects, the vultures, the pigs with human
cortexes, the liolams with human hair, and soon the part cat, part bird superhero of
a new graphic novel, occupy the post-human tapestry of her writing.
The poetry collection, The Animals in That Country, was first to set Atwood's bestiary
in motion.
In this country, wrote the poet back in 1967, the animals have the faces of people.
In Margaret Atwood's post-millennial writing, fueled to some extent by the repercussions
of 9-11 and the very real threats of bioterrorism, ecological collapse, and the end of individual
freedoms, reached their worst and full conclusion in the dystopian novels, Oryx and Crake, The
Year of the Flood, and Matt Adam, currently being adapted for an HBO television series.
These works may recall the speculative, or as Ms. Atwood says elsewhere, blueprint fiction
of The Handmaid's Tale, the 1985 Orwellian depiction of a patriarchal totalitarian theocracy
made into a motion picture film in 1985 and an opera in 2000.
To return to the 1960s, the Encyclopedia Britannica expresses it well when it remarks
how Atwood's early poetry, one, pondered human behavior, two, celebrated the natural world,
and three, condemned materialism, awarding the Circle Game the Governor General's Award
for Poetry in 1966.
These three objects of contemplation have figured in all of Margaret Atwood's writing
since, often with clinical precision and biting wit, and through the use of stunning
imagery and symbolism.
Human behavior has extended in the more recent Matt Adam trilogy to the realm of the multicolored,
genetically designed Crakers.
The natural world has been shown to collapse under the weight of the worst apocalyptic
perils of environmental abuse and capitalist greed.
As for materialism, the recent novel, The Heart Goes Last, shows it escalating into
a hair-raising confrontation between free will and biopolitical state surveillance.
For an appreciation of Margaret Atwood's sharp social pen or keyboard, her readers can and
do look as well to her non-fiction.
It is no wonder that she boasts 1.13 million followers on Twitter.
Now, apparently, and this may come as a shock to many of us here, I know, the Canadian Literature
Centre is not the only fantastic, forward-looking organization to invite Margaret Atwood to
grace its lecture series.
Negotiating with the dead, a writer on writing, grew out of a series of lectures delivered
at the University of Cambridge.
Payback, an impassioned CBC Massey lecture, treats personal and governmental debt as a
cultural issue.
And in Other Worlds, SF and the Human Imagination, Atwood meditates on science fiction.
Nor has Margaret Atwood shied away from current politics, including hot issues such as prison
farms, oil, the G20 Toronto arrests, carbon reduction treaties, and the social importance
of the arts. And then, of course, there was Stephen Harper. The policies, the
statements, the hair. Sometimes I do wonder if there is a small, tiny, secret
part of Margaret Atwood that misses Stephen Harper and the sheer amount of
good material he provided to this political witch and commentator.
Margaret Atwood is a seer, not a moralist. She's an ethicist, perhaps, showing that
to care, and care itself, may indeed not only teach us to survive, but to save
ourselves and one another. The values of solidarity, forgiveness, and preservation,
indeed of precarious lives, run throughout Atwood's storylines. They are
what propel her engagement as a writer and as a citizen. Just take the earth's
temperature, as Margaret Atwood has been doing these past decades. Instead of
turning life into gold, she urges, we have the chance to turn gold back into life.
Good water, fine air, healthy soil, clean energy. Margaret Atwood will not tell us
that the world is safe. She tells us to care that it is not. Governors and deans,
members of the CLC Executive Committee, Dr. and Mrs. Schloss, friends and
supporters, students and colleagues, please welcome to the stage, Margaret Atwood.
Thank you very much. I feel extremely well summarized. It's a pleasure to be
here. Greetings Edmontonians of 2016, among whom are some very, very old
friends indeed. Thank you for inviting me to be your Henry Kreisel Lecturer for
this year. When I was living in Edmonton from 1968 to 1970, I was old enough to
have heard of Henry Kreisel, but too young and inconsequential to actually
meet him. He had an extraordinary life and I'm happy to be delivering in his
honor this lecture, or rambling talk, or mixed bag of shards collected from the
ruins of time. When the proposal was first made to me, I resisted. I was
dragged, kicking and screaming, or at least whimpering feebly. I protested that
at my age I was getting beyond the lecture thing, and that my brain was
increasingly full of discarded sardine tins, crumpled sheets of waste paper, and
empty toothpaste tubes. But the organizers assured me that since they
were my sardine tins, waste papers and toothpaste tubes, and since I was iconic
and all, and since my work was even taught in high schools, that anything I
might bleep forth would be received with polite attention. I'm holding them to
that, and I'm holding you to it as well. No rotten tomatoes, please. This is not
1970, when things were much rowdier. In 1970, I used to get complaints about my
hair, and be told that I wrote like a housewife, fighting words. I'll mention
here that being iconic is not the walk in the park you might suppose. Once upon
a time, all you had to do as an icon was stand around looking wise, or possibly
made of wood. But nowadays, you have to smile while people take selfies with you.
It's a workout, which illustrates one of my points. Times change, and as they
change, so do the definitions and self-definitions of artists and writers.
Who knew what a selfie was in 1960? Nobody. The term had not yet been
invented. That being the case, here is the bare-naked truth. I come to you from a
planet far, far away, and in a distant galaxy, namely the past. Long past, Scrooge
gasped fearfully when told that he was being addressed by the ghost of
Christmas past. No, replied the ghost, your past. And it is of your past that I
will pray to you tonight, specifically of your literary past, and even more
specifically of the 1960s, that decade in which the current literary landscape of
Canada and its various infrastructures was formed from bits of cosmic
alphabetical dust, deformed by the forces of the great dark matter of the eternal
Canadian question mark. Do we exist? And if so, as what? Transformed by the grinding
action of market forces acting upon intellectual tectonic plates, and
squeezed like metamorphic verbal rock into the somewhat peculiar mountains,
valleys, lumps, tundras, and swamps we see before us today. To mix a metaphor, there
are, of course, many strands to this past, and I can't do justice to them all, but I
can follow a few of them, naturally, from my own vantage point. I was there. The
title of this speech is The Burgess Shale, The Canadian Writing Landscape of
the 1960s. What is writing? What do we mean by writer? Every writer answers that
question by his or her actual writing, its form, its content, its obsessions, its
axioms. By their fruits, ye shall know them, as one famous icon is said to have
said long ago. In 1955, I could have depended upon 95% of the audience to
identify that quote. Not anymore. I chose The Burgess Shale as a title because
that shale is a very important Western Canadian geological formation, and
therefore suitable for this Western and geologically aware audience. History, in
my view, always begins with geology anyway, because geology determines what
you can grow and extract, where you can safely build houses, and so forth. When
asked to write a 250-word overview of my topic, I came up with this. The Burgess
Shale is a geological formation discovered in the Canadian Rocky
Mountains that contains the fossils of many weird and strange early life forms,
different from, but not not unrelated to, later and existing forms. I have named my
revisitation of the Canadian writing landscape of the 1960s after it, perhaps
whimsically. That period is already fossilized, in a manner of speaking, and
it does contain many weird and strange life forms, different from, but not
unrelated to, those we see today. How did Canadian writers then see themselves and
what they were doing? Did they think of themselves as having careers, or rather
vocations? What did they mean by writer? What were the elements in their
environment that allowed them to flourish, or not? What magazines, physical
venues, publishers, bookstores, and attitudes? The generation of the 60s was
indeed instrumental in creating the writer-related institutions we see
around us today, from unions and private grant programs and prizes, to book tours
and book festivals and creative writing programs in universities. Why did we do that?
We should have been squashing them out. It's the competition. They did it then,
not for fun, but out of perceived need. Today's Canadian writing landscape would
be unrecognizable to those writing in the 60s, or mostly unrecognizable. But
just as an arthropod is still an arthropod, despite the time that's passed
since the Burgess Shale, writing is still writing, and writers are still writers,
whenever we mean by that. The prickly bits remain. That is the ground I hope to
cover, more or less. To assist me, I've drawn together some pictures via
PowerPoint. Just think, a person of my age knows how to do a PowerPoint.
So you can have something to look at when not checking your text messages on
your smartphones. Try saying that out loud in 1960. Blank stares, whispered
conversations about your mental stability, rumours about drink. So it
goes. I'd like to begin with two moments, not from the 60s, but from the 70s.
Neither one of them would have been possible in the 1950s, and both would
have been unlikely in the 1980s. Those two moments are the All-Star Eclectic
Typewriter Review, and the Writers Union's Failed Pornography Project. But
first, the All-Star Eclectic Typewriter Review took place possibly in 1974, or
maybe 1975, or it could even have been 1976. Here is the poster we did for it.
The month and the date are on the poster, but not the year. The review was a
fundraising event put on by the Assembled Prose Writers of English
Canada in aid of the Writers Union, which had just been formed in 1973. One reason
it was formed was that there were not any literary agents as such in Canada in
the 1960s, and writers really had no one to represent their interests, the
interests of writers as opposed to those of publishers, readers, and libraries. The
three latter felt in their hearts that simply being read was honour enough for
a writer. No money need be expected. The writers, on the other hand, took the
quaint position that what they did was work, and they ought to be remunerated by
those making use of it. Plus ça change. Those taking the view that writers' work is
like air to be had for the breathing now include many internet pundits, some
former members of our recently departed government, and a great many
universities, those bastions of fair dealing.
But I digress. The Writers Union was also an aftermath of the Royal Commission on
Publishing held in 1972, and the commission was response to the sale of
Ryerson Press, a venerable institution, to the US firm of McGraw-Hill in 1970,
which caused an outcry in the writing and publishing communities. Several young
writers scaled the statue of Edgerton Ryerson in Toronto and draped it in an
American flag. That photo op made the newspapers. Writing and publishing had
come to seem very important to the country's image of itself. It seemed
necessary to have a homegrown publishing industry that would publish books by
Canadians and about Canada. That wasn't self-evident in 1959, but by 1969 it was
an accepted attitude. The generation of the 60s writers had helped to create
that attitude. So in the mid-1970s, there the Union was, and as always it was in
pursuit of cash to keep itself going. The all-star eclectic typewriter review for
the very young, the title is a pun on electric typewriter, a snazzy innovation
of that time. The review was one of the Union's money-raising efforts and a
great success it was. It packed out St. Lawrence Hall for one night only. On
stage we had a range of acts, from Rudy Weeb and Andrea Schroeder singing
beautiful Mennonite duets. He's right. He's going to tell you that
thing I say is absolutely true. He was there, right? He was there, yes. To Pierre Burton
reciting his party piece, The Shooting of Dan McGrew, to Jack McClellan, the Canadian
publisher, wandering on stage in a vampire cloak and wax fangs, while Alan Holden sang
a spirited rendition of Jack the Knife. For the very young, this is a pun on Mack the
Knife from the Three Penny Opera. I myself headed up the Farley Mowat Dancers, a group
of six short women who, when outfitted in fur coats, toques, beards, and snowshoes,
for our first number lost in the barrens, looked remarkably like six short bearded Farley
Mowats. The snowshoes, however, were quite dangerous on the hardwood stage. I almost
killed us. I forget what the second Farley Mowat Dancer number was called, but it involved
kilts. My chef d'oeuvre as an impresario, however, was a piece called The Toronto Literary
Mafia, which was then a term denoting the literary critics of the three main players,
The Globe and Mail, The Toronto Star, and Saturday Night Magazine. I talked the three
dignified and skittish literary critics of the day into performing in the piece by telling
them the other two had agreed to do it. A standard director's trick. Then I had to get
them to wear black shirts, white ties, fedoras, and for Robert Fulford playing the god Fulford,
a Homburg. I also had to teach them to dance the cha-cha for the chorus. This number was
a tremendous hit, especially since two of them couldn't dance very well. Fulford just
kind of swayed back and forth, but the effort was much appreciated. That was one of our
fundraising efforts. The second one, The Pornography Project, came to nothing in and of itself.
Whose idea was it? Who knows? The concept was that following the lead of Naked Came
the Stranger, we would write a collective pornographic opus and sell it for a lot of
money. For the moderately young, Naked Came the Stranger was a 1969 literary hoax written
by 24 U.S. journalists. They set out to create a deliberately awful book with a lot of sex
in it as a way of exposing the bad taste and vulgarity of the times. They did, in fact,
produce a bestseller, thus proving their point. I should add that this was in an age still
relatively porn-free. Lady Chatterley's Lover and Joyce's Ulysses had been admitted into
Canada fairly recently, and the internet had not yet unleashed the present deluge
of contorted flesh upon the world. So risque prose was still risky business. Playboy centerfolds
were relatively demure, and the real stuff, such as it was, went on under the counter.
Why, we asked ourselves in 19-whatever it was, why should we Canadian literary writers
not produce our own Naked Came the Stranger as a fundraiser, we wondered. As it turned
out, there was a very good reason. As writers of erotica, we were generally terrible. The
majority of those who took the pledge never delivered. Of those who did, most ended up
writing parodies. One writer began in earnest. She was writing an erotic piece about a woman
having a love affair with a bear. I've got a picture of this. Very Canadian, and at least
it wasn't a moose or a beaver. But she became fascinated with the implications, and her
porn piece turned into a literary novel of high quality called Bear. The author was Marian
Engel, and she scooped the Governor General's Award for 1976. Yes, a GG winner began as
a money-raising hunk of porn. From such peculiar acorns do mighty oak trees grow. I'm happy
to report that Bear has recently had a revival due to its trashy paperback cover. Such is
often the effect of trashy paperback covers, of which more later. My own porn project attempt
turned very quickly into a literary parody. In fact, five literary parodies. It was called
Regional Romances or Across Canada by Pornograph. As you can see, the great Canadian motif of
transcontinental communication was already being incorporated into it. It dealt with
five regions, the Maritimes, Quebec, Ontario, the Prairies, and British Columbia. Each was
done in a style and with themes appropriate to the writing of its region. It contained
a lot of asterisks. The fourth section, the prairie one, was titled, As for Me and My
Grain Elevator, or Over Prairie Trolls. You will recognize it immediately as a reference
to Sinclair Ross's wonderful Prairie Depression classic, As for Me and My House, or maybe
you won't. Shall I read you a sample? All right. This is the prairie section. The heroine
of As for Me and My House was called Mrs. Bentley, and we never knew her first name.
As for Me and My Grain Elevator, Mrs. Warped was sitting at her flat, cheap, meager, narrow,
worn, provincial kitchen table. It was covered with gray linoleum. Once, the linoleum had
been a gay, lively shade of gray, sprigged with gray flowers, but the years had worn
it down so that it was now gray. Mrs. Warped sighed. The years had worn her down too, years
of snow, dust, hail, and rust, and the evil-minded gossip of the nasty, shrunken inhabitants
of the constricted little town of Sphincter, Saskatchewan. She looked into the cracked,
dwarfish mirror over the kitchen sink and sighed again. She was gray. She had once been
a fairly pretty woman, but now she looked old, old and frowzy, with fallen arches and
tired blood, and she was only 12. No wonder her husband, Spot, hardly touched her anymore.
She had tried to do her best for him. She was always telling him to change his socks,
and that he would get blood poisoning if he scratched himself like that. She felt so sorry
for him. Spot was an orphan, named by one of the nurses at the orphanage after a character
in the only book she'd ever read. For the very young Spot is the name of the dog in
the Dick and Jane reading series that I loathed as a child. It was hard being a minister,
especially in a narrow-minded place like Sphincter, saddled with a name like Spot.
Sphincter was so culturally deprived, she thought. She had once played the washboard
in a skiffle band, but there was no outlet for that kind of thing here. As she sat at
the kitchen table, trying to remember her first name, she heard Spot's study door slam. He was
always slamming his study door, even though she'd asked him not to. He did it so often,
she suspected it of being some kind of sexual perversion. She'd often wondered how he was
getting it off. It certainly wasn't with her. It was wearing down her nerves. If he did that once
more, she was tempted to kick him in the balls. She tiptoed to the study with his evening glass
of hot milk and peeped in. Spot was gone, but the window was open, and through it she could see the
endless flat prairie, which was gray in color. So that was it. Slamming the study door was a
ruse, so he could sneak out at night in search of freedom. Not that she didn't understand,
she too wanted her freedom. Many times she had longed to run along the railroad tracks,
flinging off her wedgies and white ankle socks, but you couldn't do things like that in Sphincter.
Mrs. Warped slipped through the open window. She was just in time to see Spot entering the
barn with a lantern. He must be seeing someone there. The one cow had already been fed,
so that wasn't the explanation. She would confront him and find out the truth at last.
As she peered around the corner of the barn door, an astonishing sight met her eyes. Spot
was standing in front of the cow, who looked bored and skeptical. His pose was suggestive.
His hips were tilted forward, one hand was on his chest, the other outflung in a gesture of
abandonment. As she watched, an expression of obscene pleasure transformed his face. This was
not the Spot she knew. Ontario stinks, he said in a seductive, masterful voice. So, she said,
stepping out to confront him, this is how you've been spending your evenings. Spot was surprised
to see her, but not dismayed. It's not what you think, he said. I'm just practicing. Practicing
what, she demanded. If he was going to be unfaithful, at least he could admit it like a
man. I'm leaving the ministry, he said. Sphincter is too small to hold me. I'm going into provincial
politics. Mrs. Warped sighed with relief. Everything was explained. You can see why we
never finished the project. The British Columbia entry, which lacked capital letters in that age,
of late hippiedom and Bill Bissett, contained a very filthy but titillating phrase. Filthy and
titillating in BC, which was revealed at the end. That phrase was, dare I pronounce it in polite
company, that phrase was, Ontario stinks. Across Canada by Pornograph has never been published,
but for a suitable sum, I might consider. No, what am I saying? Point being that neither of
these wrinkles in time, the review, the porn project, would have been possible at any other
moment. The review brought together Canadian writers from all age groups and from across
the country, working together and making idiots of themselves for a common cause. Canadians,
being great satirists and comedians, like making idiots of themselves for a common cause. In fact,
if you refuse to do it, you are viewed as a stuffed shirt. Europeans frequently do not
understand this national characteristic. Would you be able to put together such an event now?
Probably not. Canadian literary culture has become too vast, too diverse, and too classified into
genres. It would be hard to bring it all under one roof. As for the porn project,
no one today would even think about it.
Porn is so widespread, it's no longer of much interest as a subject, either for serious
exploration or for parody. It's just there, a condition of our modern life, like fast
food.
The outburst of cultural energy that took place in the sixties was in its turn a product
of the two decades that came before. It's always difficult for young people to see their
own time in perspective. When you're in your teens, a decade earlier seems like ancient
history, and your own time seems normal. What is now is surely what has always been. But
as time moves on, you realize that isn't true. Your own first decades are just as much of
a snapshot in time as other decades were.
What came before the sixties were the fifties, and what came before the fifties were the
forties. And in the forties was World War II, which in retrospect marked a huge rift
in the cultural life of Canada. Before the war, Canadians thought of themselves as part
of the British Empire. So did they during the war, and for perhaps five or six years
after it. But by the mid-sixties, they didn't so much, hardly not at all. The empire itself
had fallen apart, and the new empire, the American one, was in the ascendant.
When writing a novel set in real time, rather than in the future, I make a chart that has
the months of the year down the left-hand side, and the years across the top. I can
then place the birth dates of the characters exactly, and I will always know how old these
people are, both in relation to the other characters, and also in relation to the world
events and the national and even local ones that were taking place. I will now do that
for myself. I was born in 1939, November, thus two months after the war began. I was
thus of an age to remember the end of the war, though not its beginning. This is what
my early life looked like. It was somewhat unusual in that it took place out in the bush
without electricity or running water, but it was not as unusual then as it would be
now. A lot of people still lived like that. So this is northern Quebec. That is a team
of horses for the very young. Those are horses. That is a sledge. We're going down the lake
in a sledge in the winter on the ice. Remember ice? So I'm that person in the blue snowsuit
in the middle. That is the same like I'm the person in the sun bonnet. That is my mother
who was luckily a tomboy and really liked going out into the bush. She preferred it
to being in the city, being a person who disliked hats and gloves. And that is, remember that?
That's a camera. That's the first camera, the kind that had a leather case. And I think
some of those were, that camera even took some early Kodachrome colored pictures. Wow.
That was an innovation. That's my dad. His past is that he grew up in the backwoods of
Fort Nova, Scotia on a very small backwoods farm without electricity or running water.
And therefore he knew how to do all of those kinds of bush things. And he also knew how
to do carpentry. So every house we lived in up there he built himself. And if you needed
a piece of furniture he would just build it. Not mahogany, but it was functional. This
is the energy supply. And there it is in action. So we did our cooking on a wood stove.
And every summer I still do some cooking on a wood stove just to show that I can still
get my hand in. I make pies on a wood stove. What kind of pies you well may ask. Blueberry
pies. And sometimes rhubarb pies. And sometimes, never mind. So fish was an important protein
source, especially during the war. The other protein source was Spam. Remember Spam? For
the very young, Spam was something that came in a tin. It wasn't the stuff that turns up
on your computer in the form of ads. So it's actually very good if you're living in the
bush and cooking it on a wood stove with bacon. So I learned to fish quite early. Those are my
own fish. I caught them. They are bass. I have them on a stringer. I'm making that face not
because I'm sorry for the fish, but because they smell like fish. So I would be about,
I'd be about five at that point. So these are a few images that some of the less young may
recognize. We had these up in the woods because they didn't go bad. It was very important. We
had no refrigerator. So we had carnation milk in a tin. I like carnation milk. Trappers like
carnation milk. We had Klim. You will recognize at once that that is milk spelled backwards.
It's a powdered whole milk and you put it in a bowl and with your egg beater you whipped it up
and there were always lumps. The lumps were the best. Just love those lumps. And Crisco. Where
would we have been without Crisco? I still make my pies with Crisco. I know it's bad for you,
but I don't eat that many of them. So that's what we did. And to keep things cold, we had an ice
house. And sometimes we had a garbage pail sunk in the ground, which will give you a moderate
shade of coolness, but not as good as the ice house. For the very young, an ice house was a
building. And in the winter, you sawed blocks of ice out of your ice source. And you used an
ice saw. Specialized saw for sawing the blocks of ice. You put them in the ice house and you
covered them with sawdust. And they would last that way, if they were big enough, pretty much
all summer. And you put the things to be kept cold on top of the ice. Did anybody ever actually do
that? Yeah. See? You see, I'm not lying. It happened. It happened. Once upon a time, there
was no television. There was no internet. When you were ill, you were given a scrapbook, some
old magazines, some scissors, and some LePage's mucilage, which is a kind of glue. And you cut
the interesting parts out of the magazines and pasted them in. And the interesting parts were
always the ads. So we were early McLuhanites. We understood this stuff before McLuhan did,
although I expect he got his own start in the very same way. So these are some wartime things
from magazines pasted into my scrapbook. My mother kept some of this stuff. We were great
drawers and writers because there was nothing else to do when it rained. Drawing, writing,
and reading, that's what we did. So my brother wrote great war epics. And once in a while,
I put some war into mine, which featured flying rabbits. And you can see that they're having a
war in one of these panels. They're smiling eerily while they're doing it. And those are the bullets
coming out of their guns. And in the other panel, they're just twirling. My superheroes did a lot of
twirling. That's their castle. So then you can tell when this is because there's a lot of mud.
It's post-war. There was a big post-war housing boom, and that involved a lot of mud. So there I
am in my post-war outfit. By this time, I'm living in, you know, the bad place. I'm living in Toronto
in the winters. But we're still in the north in the summers, and here I am doing the kind of
drawing that you saw right at the beginning, entertaining my younger sister. No electricity,
kerosene lamp. And so it went. By this time, we're in the 50s, and we're into the age of short shorts.
Maybe I'll go back. I won't let you look at that picture while I'm telling you about the 50s. You
can look at this one. Then came the 50s and McCarthyism. Canadian writing of the 40s had
naturally enough focused on the themes at that time. I grew up thinking that there hadn't been
any Canadian writing much. We weren't taught it as a subject in school, and though the odd
Canadian thing made its way in amongst the Thomas Hardy and Shakespeare and George Eliot and John
Keats, but Gwethelyn Graham wrote a huge bestseller, Earth and High Heaven in 1944, featuring a romance
between a Jewish man and a non-Jewish woman. It topped the New York Times chart for over six
months, and Gabrielle Roy wrote an even bigger one, The Tin Flute, in 1946. Its first American
print run was a staggering 700,000 copies. That doesn't happen anymore. We high schoolers, however,
knew nothing of either of these because the climate had changed so completely in the early
1950s. By the time I hit high school, the Cold War was in force. Annihilation by atomic bomb
hovered as a fearful possibility, and McCarthyism had made any talk of social welfare or workers'
rights sound suspiciously like communism. Catchwords that had been all the rage in the
1930s and the 1940s were now out. You couldn't say working class or even world peace anymore
without attracting suspicious glances. Thus, Gabrielle Roy's masterpiece must have seemed
dangerously communistic to nervous educators of the 1950s. Not only did it blurt out the working
class right on its 1947 American edition flyleaf, but it talked energetically about economic and
social inequalities, and its most idealistic character looked forward to, and I quote,
a just society. After Roy, we'd have to wait for Pierre Trudeau's leadership speech in 1968 to
hear this phrase spoken so publicly again. In the 1950s, just society would simply have sounded too
communist. Odd to remember this now when the 1% and the theme of income equalization have risen
into view again, but that's how it was. Another change had happened, this time in the means of
distribution. The cheap paperback had appeared, and it wiped out the vestiges of the old Canadian
publishing system that had flourished in earlier decades, selling cheap hardback editions. The new
paperback houses were owned in the States, so you could buy a classic by Hemingway with a trashy
cover on it in the corner drugstore. I expect a lot of kids were lured into good reading in that
way, but such books were very unlikely to be Canadian. Similarly, there was no Canadian film
industry then. There was hardly any professional theater. The touring companies that used to visit
Canada having been knocked out by the war. There was no Canadian opera company. There was the Royal
Winnipeg Ballet, and there were a couple of symphony orchestras. There was jazz here and there. There
was the CBC radio, arguably more vigorous and eccentric than it since became. Television was in
its early stages. Most television watched at that time was American network television, although Don
Messer and his Islanders were a great unifying force. First we had them on the radio, and then
they had a television show, and if you go back and look at some of those clips, there are some
amazing step dancing going on. Wasn't a cultural wasteland Canada-wise? Not exactly. I'd call it a
decade of robust amateurism and do-it-yourselfery. Little theaters were numerous, and putting on
skits and making fun of things was a widespread practice. This is me dressed as a hot dog,
1954. I made that outfit myself. Why? Why couldn't I have been a clown like everybody else? The thing
on my head is the part of my
mustard. I'm the one who isn't the clown. You did make a lot of things yourself. I made
that skirt. I made these outfits for the world's only home economics opera. The letters denote
the fabrics, ourlon, nylon, and dacron, who were the daughters of old King Cole, C-O-A-L,
because it had to have a real home economics content to it, right? People still turn up
here and there who can sing the songs. I was told by Richard Bradshaw of the Canadian Opera
Company that I absolutely ruined Hoffman's Barcarolle for him by singing the washing
song from the home economics opera to him in an elevator. It was set to that tune.
I made that, too. If you fooled around in the arts, you frequently found yourself multitasking,
acting one moment, painting sets the next. These qualities came in handy when it was
time for a bunch of kids who didn't know what they were doing to start new publishing
companies in Canada, which we did in the 1960s. Due to demographics, low birth rates in the
30s due to the depression and also during the war, followed by the baby boom, those
born in the middle 30s to the middle 40s found that their services, whatever they were, were
much in demand. We stepped into a relative emptiness. We sought to fill it. We didn't
see why not. Here we go, getting that hair under control. This is a bit of the intellectual
climate. That's Marcia McLuhan's first book. It was called The Mechanical Bride, and he was
cutting ads out, pasting them into the scrapbook of this book, and writing some very funny
commentary on them. He was forced to withdraw the book because he didn't get permission
from the advertisers. He didn't clear copyright, but you could buy copies of it out of his
cellar in the late 50s, so of course I did. I noticed that it's since come back into print,
so he must have been able to, or somebody must have been able to clear the copyright.
That was the first book, The Gutenberg Galaxy, which is really prophetic. He would be right
at home in the internet age. He'd have a lot to say about it. It came along, I think, in
62. The other big book was Fry's Anatomy of Criticism, which had a big effect in many
ways in many parts of the world. There it was. We were undergraduates. What did we know?
We thought it was just normal life.
This is the coffeehouse movement of the late 50s, early 60s. That is Sylvia Tyson. She's
on guitar because on poetry nights there was always a musical interlude. You got folk songs
about girls being murdered. It was the usual theme. I'm the pensive person in the background
in the middle. That was where I first started reading my poetry out loud.
The 60s was a time when poetry in Canada had an importance that it probably hasn't had
since, partly because it was so hard to get novels published in Canada then. Reading poetry
aloud, starting with the beats in the City Lights bookstore in San Francisco, had become
the coffeehouse movement. That's where the likes of me got our starts.
These are the two guys who put it on. Don Cullen ran the whole Bohemian Embassy thing,
which was in, of course, a condemned warehouse with exposed brick walls and candy bottles
on little tables. He had a folk singing night, a jazz night and various other nights. John
Robert Columbo, who has since gone on to write 200 books, was the poetry impresario. He was
the person who would invite you to read.
You're going to be seeing the pictures of some of the people who read there.
Among the young writers I knew, most were poets. Some had prose ambitions. None thought
they could make a living from their writing alone. The Morley Callahan model, go to the
states, write stories for the glossy magazines, seemed out of our reach. The kind of writing
we wanted to do was experimental, quirky, non-commercial. We had very scant notions
about the market or how to sell anything, how to sell things or any of that. That's
Milton Acorn, really a very good poet. Believe it or not, those two people got married. It
didn't last very long, but they did.
There's Al Purdy, an early picture of him. He was one of those people. What we were reading
was two poets who had already got large audiences. One of them was Irving Leighton and the other
one was Leonard Cohen, who was not yet known as a musician and singer. He was known as
a poet. There was young Leonard. All of this time I was doing the kind of thing I've always
done, which is illustration and drawing and stuff like that. As I say, if you're in the
arts, you did it all. You filled in when you needed a picture. If they needed a picture,
you made a picture. If they needed more writers with different names, you wrote something
and put a different name on it. Make it look as if more people were actually interested
in writing than were. There actually weren't very many at that time. We did not think
of writing as a career, but as a vocation, a calling. Sheila Mautzen and Marie Claire
Blay were the kind of thing we had in mind, non-traditional novels, both of them. There's
Marie Claire Blay. She published Mad Shadows in 1959 at the age of 19. That was pretty
challenging for me. I was already more than 19 by the time I read it. I read her first
book in 1961. I was 21. It was my fourth and final year at Victoria College at the University
of Toronto. I was in a course called English Language and Literature, which took the student
from Anglo-Saxon to T.S. Eliot, covering everything in between. At the very end, as a sort of
dessert, we were allowed a course in the modern novel. At the very end of that, as a sort
of double espresso, we were given two books by Canadians, The Double Hook by Sheila Mautzen
and Mad Shadows, which was the English translation of La Belbette by Marie Claire Blay. We didn't
study Canadian literature as such in Englang and Lit, so these books weren't on the course
because they were specifically Canadian. I think they were chosen because of their
unconventional forms. In the case of The Double Hook, with its terse, intercut short sequences,
you might say modernist. Mad Shadows was a supercharged, anti-fairy tale of jealousy,
narcissism, and revenge, written by a 19-year-old and praised by Edmund Wilson. It was a formidable
role model for a similarly aged young female writer such as myself. This is my first published
book. It is indeed Double Persephone, and I did hand set it on a flatbed press. They
had a shortage of A's. I can only do one poet at a time, and who owned that flatbed press?
It was indeed John Robert Columbo, of whom I have just spoken. The cover was made out
of a lino block, so I chiseled that out myself with my little lino block tool, and I set
it into the flatbed press and ran it through. I made a bad mistake. Instead of hand sewing
the pages in, I rubber cemented them. It dries out. You'll know you have one of the old
and the original once the middle falls out of it. I only made about 100 of them, I think.
I actually went around to bookstores and got them to place it in their store, in the magazine
section. What did it cost? Fifty cents. If only I had kept more of them, but who knows?
This is James Rainey. He ran a magazine called Alphabet, and he also started out hand setting
it. You can't imagine it was prose. He did the whole thing. He had people helping him,
but he hand set it in letterpress. This is a young Michael Ondaatje, pre-beard. I think
he must have been about 18, 19 at that moment. I met him through Doug Jones, who has just
died, and was the author of the first big study of Canadian literature, which was called
Butterfly on Rock. It was an academic study, but it was there. Michael through Doug and
Kim, who was soon to become Ken Ondaatje. Doug was 36 at the time, unlike in this photo.
In 1964 or 5, I'm in Vancouver, working on the very lowest rung of the English department,
teaching grammar to engineering students. 8.30 in the morning in a Quonset hut. We got
along just fine. Why was that? I got them to read Kafka's parables. Engineers like parables,
and to write parables of their own, and then we could talk about the grammar. That's not
my motorcycle. Worst luck. There I met Bill Bissett under a table. Who does not know what
a happening was? Just about everyone, I think. A happening involves drobe lights. That's
about all I can remember about it. I was looking at something that was on a table at the happening,
and this furry thing crawled down in the vicinity of my legs. It was Bill Bissett. He is by
the way my astral twin. We were born on the same day of the same month of the same year.
Inside Bill Bissett, there's a small Margaret Atwood, and inside Margaret Atwood, there's
a tiny Bill Bissett. Think of it as a sort of yin and yang thing. I also met John Neelove,
a very fine early Prairies poet who was in Vancouver at the time. If you never have come
across this first book, Moving and Alone, have a look. I also met Jane Rule, who had
just published Desert of the Heart, the first lesbian novel that doesn't turn out badly.
She and Helen Suntoff recommended that I read The Stone Angel by Margaret Lawrence, which
had just been published. That was very impressive for me to read that book in 1964.
My own first real book that I didn't handset myself was published in 1966. This is the
first one. It's the Contact Press cover. I made that cover myself out of lattre set
and those stick-on legal dots. It was cheap. That's why we did a lot of things in those
days. It's because they were cheap. As poets in the early 1960s, we didn't expect to sell
more than a couple of hundred copies, if that. We published in small magazines, both in Canada
and the United States and sometimes even in England. It was a much smaller world then.
Poets knew one another and often began magazines and edited them. I've already spoken of James
Rainey's magazine Alphabet. He himself was one of the quirkier Canadian talents. A Suit
of Nettles, 1958, is a series of Spencerian eclogues delivered by the members of a fleet
of geese. When you come to think of it, this is remarkably odd. The decade abounds with
similar oddities. Larry Garber's story, Death by Toilet, Joe Rosenblatt's Easter Egg Poems,
the sound poetry of Bill Bissett and also that of the Four Horsemen, the beginning of
spoken poetry movement. None of this was aimed at the general reading public, though the
general reading public often became interested in it, for in the mid-60s, the newspapers
noticed the fact that these strange things were happening and began to write about them.
Even members of Parliament noticed and denounced poets from the floor of the House. Those were
the days. This is Robert Weaver, who ran a program on the CBC called Anthology. He knew
everybody. He used to go across the country, seek out young writers, all writers, and broadcast
their work on the radio. He actually paid money. It was very encouraging to have somebody
who would actually do that.
Élis Monroe owes her start to him as well. He's the glue. You're looking at the glue right there.
We couldn't always travel from coast to coast, but he could, and he was a fantastic gossip.
So we heard stories about one another before we even met one another.
It did say, printed in Canada on Canadian paper by mindless acid freaks.
So it began appearing. It has continued on to this very day.
It's gone through various incarnations, but this is the beginning of it in the 60s.
It was able to do that because Stan owned the press, so he didn't have to pay anybody to print these books.
and make other income by printing random assorted printing jobs.
This is James Rainey's book that I was telling you about.
In 1967, an old college pal of mine from England and lit,
Dennis Lee and Dave Godfrey founded the House of Anansi Press.
And how did they get the money for it?
Dennis got grants for the first four writers and then persuaded the writers to kick their grant money
into shares in the publishing company.
That's how he did it. The grants were enormous. They were like $650 each.
Their headquarters was, of course, in a cellar.
A lot of these things had cellars involved in them.
And their one employee, Doug, now George Feathering, wrote about that experience
in this book of his called Travels by Night.
That's him on the front in the 60s.
This is a little bit later.
Time happens.
So this is my first jacket photo for the Circle Game.
It was taken by the artist Charles Pachter.
You can see how serious I look.
Very poetic.
This picture, however, was from the same photo shoot.
And here's Charles when he had hair, also from the same shoot.
He knew that hair was going, so he incorporated some of it into the title page of the handmade paper
for his first handmade book, which was a sequence from the Circle Game.
Made out of his blue jeans and his hair.
As they say, it was a do-it-yourself generation.
Some years later, here is the Illustrated Journals of Susanna Moody,
which is the latest version of our joint illustrated book that we did, first of all, in those days.
This is my first newspaper interview picture.
It was for the Circle Game. I'm in Cambridge, Massachusetts, and it's 1967.
It's the age of the miniskirt.
The person who did the interview had just come back from the Vietnam War.
He was a combat journalist, and he was told that since he was in Boston,
he should interview this young female poet.
We sat there looking at each other, and he finally said,
Say something interesting.
Say you write all of your poetry on drugs.
And so it goes.
There I am in 1967 with Mordecai Richler.
The pantsuit has appeared.
It is the year of Expo 67.
These were the years when Jack McClelland of McClelland and Stewart,
at that time a Canadian-owned publishing house,
was throwing his weight big time behind Canadian writing.
This was partly, again, a function of changes in publishing.
High schools were scrapping the set curriculum,
thus jerking the rug out from under the publishers
that had depended on that steady and dependable market,
and Jack saw an opening and went for it.
I don't publish books. I publish authors, he said to me,
at the moment when he took me on.
It was 1967. I was wearing a Mao jacket.
You can see it right there.
And maybe it was the jacket that convinced him I was a viable literary commodity.
To support his Canadian authors, he invented the Cross Canada Book Tour.
He did book tours before U.S. publishing took them up,
and he was well known for being willing to do anything to promote a book.
It was he who, to promote a book on snakes,
sent out a number of boxes with breathing holes in them
to all the book reviewers, triggering a rash of screaming
and frantic calls to the Humane Society
by editors who thought the snakes must have gone out.
To promote a book on football, it was miniature jockstraps.
It should be said that he and many of the writers of his own age
that he so adored, such as Farley Mowat,
were veterans of World War II.
We kids must have seemed pretty tame to them, come to think of it.
This is, again, Montreal.
There's Al.
The maxi coat has also arrived for the very young.
The fashion was to wear the maxi coat with a mini skirt under it.
Figure that out.
And with that, you wore, of course, your knee-high go-go boots.
I met George and Angela Bowering at that time.
They were in Montreal.
That's young George.
Marion Engel, around that time, published No Clouds of Glory.
It was she who later wrote Bear.
That's Edmonton, 1968 to 70.
I was here when my first novel was published.
It was The Edible Woman.
And I did do my first public signing
in the Hudson's Bay Department in Edmonton, Alberta,
in the Men's Sock and Underwear Department.
I do not know why that particular spot was chosen.
I think it was beside the escalator.
And I think the idea was that people coming up and down the escalator
would see me sitting there with my little pile of books
amongst the jockey shorts called The Edible Woman
and would then come and purchase it.
But that did not happen.
I simply caused a stampede amongst all the men
who were there to buy their socks at lunch hour.
They saw this and ran flippity-flop in their galoshes
in the opposite direction.
I only sold two copies.
It was very discouraging.
Luckily, Mel Herdig had a bookstore here at that time.
And if you felt discouraged,
you could just drop in and visit Mel,
and he'd give you an earful.
And he has been giving you an earful ever since.
Sometimes he still gives me an earful over the phone.
So that was quite wonderful.
That is my Edmonton outfit, courtesy the Sally Ann.
The bottom part is the actual used mink coat.
Sheared beaver, I think it is.
And the top part was a shruggy.
Do you know what a shruggy was?
Well, it was little short ones that people wore with evening gowns in the 1940s.
And I got a hold of one of those, and I made it into a hat.
Cut off the sleeves, sew it up a bit, put in a hook.
It was very warm.
And at this point I met Pat Lane.
Poets used to go around and visit each other.
Prose writers didn't know one another yet, but the poets did.
So a poet would turn up in your city and sleep on your floor.
So Pat Lane slept on my floor in Edmonton.
Imagine that.
And I slept on Alice Monroe's floor in Victoria.
Because she had just published Dance of the Happy Shades in 1968.
I've known her ever since that time.
This is my jacket photo for The Edible Woman.
It's the same outfit you just saw, but from a slightly foxier angle.
And this is the cover of that first novel,
and it was done by Charles Pachter with hair.
At the same time, Graham Gibson publishes Five Legs in 1969,
and it outsells Valley of the Dolls for at least a week.
But a week, you know, something.
I met him, the author and canoeist, at a party for Milton Acorn in 1970.
Why were we having a party for Milton Acorn?
Because he had not won the Governor General's Award,
but his ex-wife Gwendolyn McEwen had.
As a result of which, for some unknown reason,
he attacked George Bowering.
There were a lot of feuds going on in those days,
but that was an odd one.
Anyway, he was very discouraged,
so we gave him something called the People's Poets Award,
which cheered him up enormously.
I was wearing a white crocheted mini dress with wooden buttons.
You remember these things.
At the same time, along comes the women's movement
radiating outwards from New York.
We had kind of already had it for a while
because we had Doris Anderson, a prairie girl,
and she had been the editor of Chatelaine since 1957,
and she had been covering a lot of the issues
that the United States belatedly discovered were of interest.
This is when the hair appears again.
I go to England, France, and Italy,
partly as the result of a phone call that I got
while I was in Edmonton, and the phone call went like this.
Hello, is this Margaret Atwood?
This is Oscar Lewenstein from London, England,
and we would like to option your novel, The Edible Woman.
What was I doing?
I was doing a paint job on my toilet seat
when I got that call.
And I said, who is this really?
Well, it really was Oscar Lewenstein.
We ended up working on a script for The Edible Woman
with Tony Richardson.
It never got made, but it was lots of fun to work on it.
There's Tony right there, and that is Natasha as a child.
Then I come back to Canada.
I join the Anansi team as a board member and editor,
and I edit for them, and I publish surfacing,
and I also publish my book on Canadian literature called Survival.
And the true history of that is that, like everybody in small publishing,
we were sitting around figuring out how to make enough money
to support the groundbreaking experimental novels
and first poets that we were putting out there.
And we had been doing a line of,
you know the books called The Idiot's Guide To?
We were first on the ground with that.
We did a book called Law, Law, Law, which was basically
The Idiot's Guide To How to Write Your Own Divorce
and Your Will and this and that.
Then we did the first popular guide to venereal disease,
which was called VD.
And we got as far as warts.
AIDS hadn't manifested itself yet, so we didn't do that.
We had quite a success with those, so what were we going to do next?
And I said, there isn't a popular book on Canadian literature
for the general reader.
Why don't we do a sort of VD of Canadian literature?
And so we did.
And that was Survival.
There it is, the VD of Can Lit.
And that set the cat amongst the pigeons
because all of a sudden it was an important subject.
We thought we would sell maybe 5,000 of them.
How many did we ultimately sell?
We sold 100,000 of them.
And we did this for a long, long time.
But everybody, of course, once I'd done it,
felt I should have done it differently.
No doubt, no doubt.
Had I had longer than four months to do it,
maybe I would have done it differently.
There was something missing in the Can Lit landscape of that time.
I mention that in the second, in fact, I devoted a whole second chapter to it
because at the time I was writing Survival,
although Canadian literature had started out with people
writing about First Nations themes
and with First Nations authors,
Wakusta, indeed, Pauline Johnson,
and writing about, there's Mara's long narrative poem,
Tecumseh, from the 19th century.
People had been doing that,
but they weren't doing it anymore at that time.
And there were no First Nations authors of poems, plays, or novels.
That hadn't happened yet.
I predicted in Survival that it would, and now it has.
And one of the first things to happen was that people,
beginning with the poets but continuing with, for instance, Rudy Weeb,
who is right here, turned back to those parts of history
and went into them and looked at what had really gone on.
They looked at some of the things that you never learned about in school
in those days and brought them to the surface again.
And we now have an idea as to why there were no...
people writing in that period,
and it has a lot to do with the residential school system
and the attempt to deculturate First Nations and Inuit people.
But now we are living in an age in which those writers
